en_body:
Tonight's black shadow breathes a slight cold
Fear suddenly seeps slowly through
Love is no longer enough
To cradle me within Blackness...
Now Blackness hides and sinks
Light flickers with will-o'-the-wisp shadows.
My back is bare, my cheeks are yellow
My dazzled eyes glisten with gathering pus
I have grown old,
Now truly, it is
I have grown old!
Old people often grow infirm, hard to please and hard to soothe
There will be hands without patience
striking their buttocks or slapping their faces.
They will not tell because their children and grandchildren do not wish to hear or know
Only the nurses on duty, especially upon the night watch, "the graveyard ship"
They often have those impatient hands lying in wait!
...As Time is lying in wait to carry me into a nursing home
And I shall see truly and most truthfully: those hands!
And my children and grandchildren will not believe because they do not wish to hear or know...
...Out there the darkness has not yet flooded over them.
They still have "deadlines" and Monday Night Football or weeknight sitcoms: waiting before the evening table
Before locking the doors, turning out the lights
And sleeping peacefully upon their cream-colored mattress.
(Midnight, Wednesday, 15/03/2000, Shreveport, LA, USA)
note: The quoted phrase “the graveyard ship” is preserved from the poet's English in the Vietnamese text, where it evokes the overnight nursing shift.
